item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1, very good.

I had a problem with the following:

Maybe not ideas themselves, but they are the germinal form of all living things and actions. Once you, Katherine, were just a thought.

Simply because I fundamentally disagree with the concept you present, almost, but not quite enough to downvote the article because of it. Then I realized this is the subtlest of 4th wall breaks…
…as Cyllus, Drumel, and Wells… in fact all of Foundationverse gestate as thoughts before being brought to life as stories, and only live those lives when they are read by YOU. hashtagpataphysics

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It makes me thinking about the stories in my childhood, books which tell their own stories, libraries become alive. Thank you for this article and the beautiful dream you've told.

(well, perhaps what I've mentioned is slightly different from the original idea lol)

item.title: Re: Really enjoyed this.
item.content:encoded:
I see. So the Operation Reading Rainbow is to gather the books inside the Foundation to recreate the skip; the Operation LeVar is to read the books in the Library of Congress to make them happy.

I was guessing the same way… Anyway, the Hear No Evil part is also well developed, and I'm looking forward to your if ever article featuring a blind MTF.

item.title: Re: Really enjoyed this.
item.content:encoded:
1. Never explicitly explained, but implied in Addendum 3a (last paragraph); Operation LeVar is meant to implant Foundation operatives in the Library of Congress "to read all eligible texts in the library, and continue to do so daily for the incoming additions that fit the conditions to contribute to the phenomenon." (May be obvious, but it is named for the host of Reading Rainbow.)
  2. Because I'm an airhead; the sensory-impaired MTF featured here is deaf, not blind. Hate that I'm airing out that I made that error and that it took me so long to catch it, but I'm glad I caught at least. The gimmick would still sorta work I guess as a very general play on sensory-impaired MTFs, but it's a better fit for an article that actually features a blind MTF. So that oversight and courtesy are why it's changed. I'd use it if I ever wrote an article featuring a blind MTF.

item.title: Really enjoyed this.
item.content:encoded:
My favorite types of skip are that of alternative history and that of humanistic emotion under scientific statements. This skip has a touch of both of them, so I enjoyed it very much.
Also I like the lovely pun in the title. An easy +1.

However, I have several questions while reading.

  • What is Operation LeVar?
  • For the title of this skip, it was braille for "We Become Stories That Are Dying To Be Told". And now with the update I found it turned into just that. Can you elaborate a bit on both of these decisions?

item.title: Author here
item.content:encoded:
This is a well-conveyed reason for not liking the article, and that you figured it out before the last line is a testament to you. You're right that the article leaves no room for interpretation or ambiguity; the explicit delivery of the last line was the initial motivation for the article and is meant to drive the premise beyond in-universe, ideally being recalled during everyday life when amid rows of countless books somewhere.

I feel like a majority of the best articles here can be described as either:

I agree that this article could stand to take notes from the site's best, and even have that ambition to begin with, but I can't agree that the article is trying to be the first bullet point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid I liked the idea of this one more than the execution and had to downvote accordingly.

My main gripe is that you have an extremely long series of explorations recorded seeming to lead us to the conclusion that you end up literally spelling out in the last few sentences of the final collapsible.

I feel like a majority of the best articles here can be described as either:

  • Short, to-the-point articles that trade exposition and world-building for elegance and brevity, or
  • Longer articles that take a basic idea and slowly build around it, to the point that the true thrust of the article lies between the lines and is realized by its completion.

This article tries to do both, and right as I finished processing everything I had slowly figured out myself reading the long article, the final collapsible simply says it in a matter-of-fact way that totally robs the article of any sense of interpretation or ambiguity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The short answers to any of the detail being incorrect are that (1, in-universe) the statement is not exact science by today's research on how we hear, but more along the lines of a belief of an expert character (also of an irl consult), and probably subject to debate among his colleagues and (2, out-of-universe) the present detail is sufficient enough for the article's purposes, and what isn't there is a willful handwave. But, I'm very interested in this kind of talk, so.

I don't know a whole lot about multilateration, and will read about it, but to add to this discussion from what's here already, this

you can use the timing between when one microphone detects a spike and when another microphone detects a spike to figure out which one is further away from the source of the sound.

is how the mammalian brain, specifically the inferior colliculus localizes sound ("interaural cues").1 It utilizes more minute cues than time and intensity discrepancies though; e.g. how the timbre of sound waves passing through our heads is modified by the time it hits the farther ear.

To stop short of a wall of text and to get to the bottom line of where I stand; an electrochemical and biological plexus that has been improved upon over millions of years by life-or-death forces is going to be superior in this case to the impressive advancement of mechanical technology we have achieved as a species, most of which is modeled after that very plexus.

Footnotes
1. The mammalian hearing system does have the equivalent of a "blind spot"; a result of anatomy and a disadvantage microphone arrays wouldn't have. However, this is overcome in the same way a blind spot would be; just move your head a bit.

item.title: Re: I found a typo.
item.content:encoded: Plural. Nice catch. (Fixed.)

item.title: I found a typo.
item.content:encoded:
Enough charge from the constant stream of supplied electricity over the last hundred years stored on a big enough capacitors could still be shedding some of the energy via sound.

Is this supposed to be singular or plural?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Fullfölja: (Chuckles) As far as the detection of a sound source goes, no technology is even remotely close or will be in the distant future to what you and I have already brought to the table; ears.

Actually, human ears pretty much suck when it comes to localizing sound. Dog ears are much better, but if you really want to get the job done quickly and cheaply, use an array of microphones and some off-the-shelf software to compare the inputs.

Since sound waves travel through air at a fixed rate of 343 m / s, you can use the timing between when one microphone detects a spike and when another microphone detects a spike to figure out which one is further away from the source of the sound. Use four microphones, and you can use triangulation techniques such as multilateration to determine the direction in 2D that the sound is coming from. Presumably, a larger array of microphones could extend this to 3D, but it might involve writing custom code.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Superb idea with great execution. An article about books that want to be read. At first it's a bit confusing for me, but after scrolling down this just blows my mind.

+1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, wow; nevermind, I didn't even think to check the source code (beg pardon!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you to those who commented and gave this an inspiring boost.

And thanks to Zyn for her review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotcha, thanks. Image source is also in the code.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is lovely. I don't really have much more to say that hasn't been covered above, but you managed to suck me in with a compellingly strange anomaly, and then spun it out into a series of well-written plot developments that took it to a natural place with an internal logic all its own. Is it particularly spectacular? No, but its low-key approach and sheer verisimiltude lends it a charisma I haven't seen from a lot of S5 SCPs.

So yeah, </gush>. +1 for being a lo-fi SCP to read books to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit I only noticed this due to shaggy's comment. I'm glad to have, though, as it was enjoyable throughout. I agree that this is very well put together, but I found the muted ending didn't work that well for me, and was the weak point in the article. The mystery of the noise in the library turning out to be the voices of the books was lackluster given the length of the buildup and a little predictable (at least in a setting where the paranormal is the norm). However, I'm still happy to +1 something this engaging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Replying to the top post just so this is close to the top (and so staff can see it): I tracked the image back to here via a reverse Google image search; it's CC 2.0 (hence compliant, and fine).

To the author: Typically, skip writers will provide a link to the image source (demonstrating that it's CC-compliant) either on the discussion page or as a link in the caption of the image itself. Just pointing this out, because it's a mistake I myself made when posting my first skip.

(Also, excellent work; hope to see more from you at some point!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, yeah — the prose in this is absurdly good; in many aspects, it's intimidatingly good. The density, polish, and clarity with which you write is outstanding throughout — I also enjoyed the slow-burn mystery of it all. Heck, even the central anomaly is really cool (there's a droning sound in the library and we have no idea where the heck it's coming from — that's a fantastic low-key hook).

I think the log lags a bit when the characters wax philosophical about Minerva and in a few other spots. There's a number of bits here I think you could trim without losing much of anything, and — as a minimalist — I'm contractually obligated to point that out. That being said, even the fatty tissue I'm seeing is still exceedingly well-written. Your prose throughout is a pleasure to read.

Also: I have absolutely no clue if the acoustic science in this article is legitimate, but it almost doesn't matter. You sold it so well that it might as well be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This absolutely shines for sheer writing quality.

Top to bottom. Every single bit of this screams authenticity — the characters speak and feel like professionals, moreso than can typically be found in an environment of ameturish writing (myself very much included). The handling of Hear No Evil was similarly top notch, and the civilian expert and their concerns came across as wholly realistic.

It's not wildly entertaining. It's a slow-burn mystery surrounding an admittedly dry subject, the handling of which resolves to be quite muted in the end. This however, strengthens the already powerful verisimilitude for me — though it is a narrative, it feels closer to what an actual SCP file would look like.

+1, and a very well-earned one at that. We need more writing of this calibre.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: no problem! (also, another thing: you can remove the 'height: 110%;', as its redundant code i forgot to cut)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hey, ive modified your addenda and small horizontal rule things to not require html blocks as html blocks kinda suck

+ show block
– hide block

include this in your page to style the two:

 [[module css]] .smaller-g hr{ width: 45%; margin: auto; background-color: #e6e6e6; height: 110%; border: none; height: 2px; } .adden{ font-size: 1.3em; } [[/module]]

use this for your addenda bit:

 [[div class="smaller-g"]] ----- [[/div]] = [[span class="adden"]]Addenda[[/span]] [[div class="smaller-g"]] ----- [[/div]]

and use this for the smaller hr:

 [[div class="smaller-g"]] ----- [[/div]]
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like the footnote outside in the series page for this slot. It's distracting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing like a good book, eh?

+1
